final_decisions.explanation: V1_REQ_006 explicitly states that ships can be placed horizontally, vertically, or diagonally during setup, so allowing vertical positioning is directly supported.
The source explicitly requires horizontal scalability to support a large number of simultaneous matches without performance degradation, which directly supports handling many games at once.
The source explicitly requires the system to detect sunk ships and notify both players when a ship is sunk, so notifying players that the ship was sunk is directly supported.

The source requirements explicitly call for a nautical/periscope presentation and ship visuals resembling actual ship classes, which reasonably supports a requirement to simulate the look of ships at sea.
The source documents contain no stated or implied requirement for transport encryption, TLS versions, or certificate pinning, so this introduces concrete security constraints beyond the evidence.

The source explicitly describes turn-based offensive/defensive roles, including a state machine with Match Turn Offense and Match Turn Defense and server-authoritative role state, which directly supports identifying one player as offensive and the other as defensive each turn.
The source documents do not mention disconnect handling, match rejoin capability, or a 5-minute reconnection window, so this adds new functionality and a specific time constraint beyond the evidence.

The source explicitly requires ship sprites or images that visually resemble actual ship classes rather than generic shapes, which directly supports displaying each ship type with a specific ship image in the grid.

The sources explicitly require that players see only their own offensive and defensive boards and prohibit visibility of any opponent board information, ship layout, shot history, or spectate/cross-player viewing.

This is directly supported by the lobby/matchmaking requirement stating that when a player clicks an available opponent, the system immediately pairs the two players and removes both from the lobby’s available list globally.

The source explicitly requires that each player place exactly five ships on their defensive board during setup at the start of a match, which directly supports allowing each player to position 5 ships on their defensive grids once the game is initiated.

The source explicitly states that after a match ends the client shall present a prominent option to 'leave the lobby entirely,' which directly supports allowing each player to remove themselves from the player list after a game ends.

The sources explicitly require a metal-on-metal impact sound for hits and immediate playback of hit sounds, which directly supports simulating a shot hitting its target when a shot is marked as a hit.

The sources require no persistent player profiles beyond session tokens and ephemeral state, but they do not state or clearly imply a specific retention rule to purge all match data within 10 minutes after a match ends.

This is supported by the source’s requirement that the client UI teach gameplay flow without a tutorial so new players can understand and complete a game, with the 9-out-of-10 phrasing being a reasonable measurable elaboration of that stated usability intent.

The sources repeatedly require anonymous lobby presence, exclusion of names/avatars/identifiers from UI and notifications, and no persistent profiles or identifying data, which directly supports protecting player identity and preventing access to identity-revealing information about other players.
This is explicitly supported by V1_REQ_006, which states that ships may be placed horizontally, vertically, or diagonally in a straight line during setup.

The source explicitly requires clear turn-start notifications of each player's current role/status to both players, including messages such as 'Your shot' or 'Brace, incoming' and visual highlights like 'Your turn' or 'Not your turn'.

The source explicitly requires that during gameplay each player simultaneously sees an offensive board/grid for tracking shots fired, so displaying an offensive grid to each player once a game is initiated is directly supported.
The source explicitly states that players must be automatically removed from the lobby list after 24 hours of inactivity, which directly supports this requirement.

The source explicitly requires a distinct longer creaking and water rushing sound for sinking ships and immediate playback behavior for the sinking sound, which directly supports simulating the sound of a sinking ship when a ship is sunk.
The sources state that the shooter selects a target cell and the server processes the shot, but they do not mention or clearly imply any additional confirmation step before submission.

The sources explicitly require that after a match ends the client present an option to relist the player as available in the lobby, which directly supports allowing each player to mark themselves available again.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_020, which states that the server shall evaluate the defender’s defensive grid to determine if the shot is a hit or a miss when processing a shot.
The sources explicitly require visual marking of sunk ships on the defender’s defensive board when a ship is sunk, including outlining the entire sunk ship and labeling it with the ship class name.
The source documents describe lobby matching, setup, and in-app notifications/localization, but do not mention background device notifications or prompting users when the app is in the background.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_047, which states the system shall provide 24x7 availability.

The source explicitly requires offensive/defensive player roles, server-authoritative role state, turn notifications of current role status, and a match state machine with Match Turn Offense and Match Turn Defense, which directly supports maintaining each player's turn status as offensive or defensive.

The source explicitly states that when a shot is resolved the shooter’s offensive grid mirrors the hit or miss mark, which directly supports marking each shot outcome on the offensive player’s offensive grid.
This is explicitly supported by V1_REQ_022, which states that when a shot is resolved the defender’s defensive grid marks the cell as hit or miss.

This is directly supported by requirements stating that sunk ships must notify both players with the ship class and that the defender’s grid must outline the sunk ship and label it with the ship class name.

The source documents describe rendering and interacting with offensive and defensive grids but never mention coordinate labels or any explicit labeling scheme along grid edges, so this adds UI detail not stated or clearly implied.
The sources specify manual ship placement rules and constraints for five ships and valid orientations, but they do not state or clearly imply any auto-place or random fleet placement control.

The source explicitly states that the match ends when a player manually ends it and that either player may manually end the match at any point, including before the first turn, which directly supports allowing a player to end a game at any time during the game.
The sources define ongoing turn processing with role/turn continuation logic and explicit end-of-game conditions, which clearly implies turns keep being assigned until the match ends.
The source explicitly requires both boards to be rendered inside a circular periscope-style viewport, which directly supports displaying the grids within a circle as a periscope view.
This is directly supported by the lobby matchmaking requirement that players can start a match by clicking an available opponent in the lobby list, which immediately pairs them.

The sources explicitly state that during gameplay each player sees two boards including a defensive board showing their own ship placements, which directly supports displaying a defensive grid to each player once a game starts.
This is directly supported by the stated turn continuation logic and role toggling requirements, which say that after a miss the shooter/defender roles swap.
The source explicitly states that idle players must be hidden from the lobby’s available list after a timeout, which directly supports making inactive players unavailable for selection.
The source documents specify audio and visual feedback for miss, hit, sink, and turn events, but they do not mention or imply device haptic feedback.
The source explicitly requires end-of-game notifications that explain the reason the match ended, including victory by sinking all ships or a player manually ending the match.
The sources explicitly require support for English, Spanish, and French and state that the client shall prompt the user to select their language immediately upon first access.

The source explicitly states a closely matching performance requirement of 95% of turn actions under 2 seconds and 100% under 4 seconds, so despite the candidate’s apparent typo ('4%' instead of '4 seconds'), its intent is supported.

The sources state that each player places exactly five ships during setup and that after both players lock in their placements the system randomly assigns which player shoots first, which directly implies defining each player’s first-turn status.
The source explicitly states that the system shall notify both players of the shot outcome (hit or miss) when a shot is resolved.
The source explicitly requires the system to maintain a lobby list/roster of players and their availability status, which directly supports maintaining a list of players.

The sources state that the match ends immediately when a player's last ship is sunk and that ships are detected as sunk when all their cells are hit, so ending the game on the hit that sinks the final remaining unsunk ship is directly implied.

The sources specify an ambient ocean sound loop during the shooter’s turn, not specifically when a shot is taken, so this wording changes the trigger and adds an unsupported event-specific audio behavior.

The source explicitly states that the shooter selects a target cell on their offensive grid when it is their turn to fire, which directly supports allowing the offensive player to define a shot each turn.
The source specifies only post-match options to relist in the lobby or leave the lobby, and does not mention any rematch feature, 30-second acceptance window, or bypassing the lobby.

The source documents define only anonymous public-lobby matchmaking and explicitly omit additional player-to-player features, with no mention or implication of private matches, join codes, or bypassing the lobby.

The sources repeatedly require anonymous lobby presence and prohibit identifying information in lobby displays, notifications, UI, logs, and player communication, which directly supports denying players access to information that could reveal another player's identity.

The source explicitly requires that sunk ships be visually marked on the shooter’s offensive board when a ship is sunk, which directly supports marking the sunk ship on the offensive player’s offensive grid.
The source explicitly states that shooter/defender roles swap when the previous shot was a miss, which directly implies the defensive player becomes offensive after a miss.

The sources consistently require anonymity and the absence of player-identifying information or persistent profiles, so allowing a user to identify themselves as a player is not supported and conflicts with the evidence.
The source explicitly requires ship placements to have no overlaps and to reject overlapping placement attempts with visual feedback during setup.
The sources explicitly require a splash sound for misses when shot outcomes occur, which directly supports simulating the sound of a shot missing its target.

The source explicitly states that after a hit the shooter may take another shot immediately and that the shooter can select a target cell on their offensive grid when it is their turn, which directly supports allowing the offensive player to define a shot after a hit.
The source explicitly states that ships may be placed horizontally, vertically, or diagonally during setup, so allowing horizontal placement is directly supported.
This is directly supported by the lobby availability toggle requirement, which states players can toggle off at any time to remove themselves immediately from the lobby’s available player list.

The source explicitly requires that the system notify both players when the match ends, including a localized end-of-game message explaining the reason (e.g., V1_REQ_037), so notifying players that the game is ended is directly supported.
The source explicitly states that only supervisors may advertise or post empty calling slots and that this access is enforced by role-based controls.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_010, which requires support for Internet Explorer and Netscape Navigator and implies browser independence across those targeted browsers.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_009, which states that most interactions shall complete within six seconds at the 90th percentile on slow (~56k) modem-speed connections, a close paraphrase of the candidate.
The source explicitly states that supervisors can enter and update caller schedules and that only supervisors can advertise empty calling slots, which directly supports both parts of the candidate.

The source explicitly includes an overlapping availability window of 7:00 AM–11:59 PM daily and also states 24/7 year-round availability, so this candidate is directly supported as a subset of the stated availability.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_008, which specifies that the supervisor schedule view page must load within two seconds at the 90th percentile and that no more than 10% of loads exceed five seconds on the DePaul TeleServices Center network.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_007, which states that new callers and supervisors shall be able to learn the system sufficiently to perform basic tasks within ten minutes of first use.
The candidate directly matches V1_REQ_002 and V1_REQ_003, which state that only supervisors may advertise empty slots and view the full roster of all callers’ schedules.

The source explicitly requires 24/7 year-round availability and also includes an overlapping availability window of 12:00 AM–6:00 PM daily, so this candidate is supported though it states only a subset of the full availability requirement.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_011, which states the portal shall achieve at least 80% positive feedback on visual appeal in beta user surveys for a representative sample of users including callers and supervisors.

The sources support supervisors viewing all callers’ schedules, but they do not mention exporting schedules, CSV output, specific columns, or filtered export behavior, so this adds unsupported functionality and detail.

The sources require schedule management, slot advertising, and role-based access, but they do not mention audit logging, immutability, deletion events, UTC timestamps, or before-and-after value capture.

The sources support supervisor schedule entry and updates, but they do not mention bulk CSV import, the specific file format and columns, overlap validation rules, or detailed error reporting for rejected rows.

The source documents discuss role-based access and portal functionality but do not mention single sign-on, Active Directory integration, or use of network credentials instead of a separate portal password.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_009, which states that on faster (~112k or higher) connections most interactions shall complete within three seconds at the 90th percentile, making the candidate a close paraphrase despite using 'all operations' instead of 'most interactions'.

The sources explicitly require ease of use/learnability for callers and supervisors and state that common/basic tasks should be completable within two minutes, so this candidate is a broadly worded but supportable restatement despite saying 'any system task' rather than 'common' tasks.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_011, which requires at least 90% positive user feedback on simplicity in beta user surveys, and by V1_REQ_012's call for a clean, minimal interface.

The sources support that only supervisors can view the full all-callers schedule and that the UI should avoid horizontal scrolling, but they do not support a print-friendly layout, a Print control, or the specific single-page US Letter landscape and 0.5-inch margin constraints.

The source documents discuss running reports and exporting them to Excel or Lotus formats, but nowhere mention emailing reports, recipient limits, corporate mail integration, or sending within the same session.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_018, which states the portal availability shall target at least 95% of IBM W3 intranet availability and treats W3 downtime as portal unavailability.

This is directly supported by the extracted requirements stating a point-and-click interaction model with full keyboard navigation and that all UI actions, selection, and navigation must be operable by both mouse and keyboard without hover-only controls.

The source documents discuss report running, exporting, access control, and security posture, but they do not state or reasonably imply immutable audit logging of executions/exports, specific logged fields, 12-month retention, or IT Security review.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_008, which requires the portal to conform to IBM and CCR branding guidelines and to display both IBM and CCR logos simultaneously.

The sources state that approved non-US CCR staff may access the portal and that reports include global regional data, but they do not mention any region-based authorization or group-scoped restriction on viewing/exporting data by assigned regions.
The source explicitly requires no automatic login or credential caching, including no cookies storing login information, which directly matches this candidate requirement.
The source explicitly states that the Insight Portal shall use CCR nomenclature consistent with existing WCS reports, matching the candidate requirement's intent.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_011, which states usability testing should aim for at least 90% first-time success without help and 90% recall of steps after a single session, matching the candidate’s intent.
The source documents mention selecting report types and filters simply, but provide no support for saved, named, deleted, or quantity-limited filter presets or a Saved Filters list.

The extracted requirements explicitly require one pre-deployment training session for all CCR users prior to go-live, and the candidate’s wording about 100% participation conditioned on user availability is a reasonable restatement of that need.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_003, which requires at least one high-level, executive-friendly summary tile or card for each report type on the results dashboard, aligning with an appealing summary for CCR management.

The source explicitly requires visible UI buttons labeled for exporting generated reports to Excel (.xls) and Lotus 1-2-3 (.123), which directly supports this candidate's spreadsheet export capability and button-based UI behavior.

The source explicitly requires use of CCR-consistent terminology in report labels, filters, and UI text and also requires usability testing with CCR users to validate terminology and overall usability before rollout.

The source explicitly requires simple report generation with a single click after selecting report type and filters, and directly visible export buttons that users can activate to export the generated report, which supports the candidate despite its informal wording.

The sources explicitly require usability testing with a goal that 90% of first-time CCR users can run a report without help and consistently emphasize usable IBM-aligned UI design, so this candidate is a reasonable synthesis despite the exact phrase 'IBM usability standards' not being stated verbatim.

The sources support access from IBM-issued Windows machines running Windows 2000 or later, but they explicitly require access only via the IBM W3 intranet and no external exposure, so 'through the Internet' is not supported and conflicts with the evidence.

The source documents discuss report generation, export formats, dashboard summaries, accessibility, and related portal behavior, but provide no evidence for user-entered notes, a 500-character limit, or inclusion of a notes worksheet in exports.
The candidate is directly supported by V1_REQ_005, which states the system shall refresh its data weekly by running scheduled queries against external WCS databases on a fixed day and time.
The source explicitly states that the portal shall include data sets from non-US regions and allow access to approved non-US CCR staff, which directly supports this requirement.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_006, which states that the Insight Portal shall support adding connections to additional designated WCS databases after initial deployment.

The candidate is directly supported by V1_REQ_013, which states that both the WCS Developer Guide and WCS User Guide must be updated to reflect every change, addition, or update to the portal and its documentation.

The source explicitly requires compatibility with IBM-issued Windows machines and full usability at a minimum resolution of 800x600, which supports the candidate’s claims about display on CCR users’ work computers and formatting for 800 by 600 resolution.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_007, which requires access to be restricted to authorized CCR staff and authentication via IBM intranet username and password.

This is directly supported by the extracted requirements stating that at least 98% of user workstations must be verified before go-live to have at least 400 MB free disk space as part of readiness requirements.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_005, which states that approximately 90% of user interactions shall respond within 2 seconds and up to 10% of heavier interactions may take up to 10 seconds.

The candidate restates the documented availability requirements of 90% year-round, 98% during budgeting, 24/7 operation, and one-hour restoration during the budgeting window after helpdesk outage reporting, despite slightly awkward wording about the 2%.

The source documents discuss UAS-based access control, data loading, UI, performance, and support processes, but they do not state or clearly imply immutable CRUD audit logging with before/after values or CSV export functionality for administrators.

The source explicitly requires integration with UAS for authentication and authorization with no local accounts or backdoors and role-based access limited to authorized users, which directly supports allowing access only to users present in UAS with RFS-assigned roles.

The source explicitly requires UAS-based authentication/authorization and role-based access mapped to PCG hierarchy levels that limit users to authorized data and functions, which directly supports this candidate.

The sources support 800x600-and-higher display, about 95% pre-approval before release, and fixing remaining UI issues within one month post-release, but not correctness in 95% of resolutions prior to implementation or correction within one week of notification.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_006, V1_REQ_007, V1_REQ_021, and V1_REQ_022, which require compliance with ENET intranet UI standards, approximately 95% Architecture group pre-approval before release, and correction of remaining UI issues within one month post-release.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_014, which states that annual revenue and expense formula changes must be applied within one week of receiving final changes from Finance and followed by two weeks of joint testing with Finance before budget opening.

The source explicitly requires that at least 98% of user workstations be verified before go-live to have IE with compatibility mode, .NET Framework 1.1, and IE Web Controls installed, so this candidate is supported despite omitting some details and using slightly different browser wording.

The source documents describe core budgeting, access control, data loading, performance, UI, and training needs, but nowhere state or clearly imply any Excel export capability, single-file output, hierarchy-preserving export format, or exact matching of exported monthly/annual totals to on-screen values.

The source documents discuss current-year budgeting data loads, access control, UI, and reliability, but provide no evidence for retaining seven years of finalized budgets, read-only historical access, or a year-selector/filtering feature for historical budgets.

The source documents do not mention edit locking, concurrent editing control, displaying another user's UAS ID, or a 10-minute inactivity timeout, so this introduces new functionality and specific constraints beyond the evidence.

The source documents emphasize core budgeting functionality, reliability, performance, and explicitly excluding new features like notifications or dashboards, but they do not state or clearly imply automatic draft autosave, timed save intervals, navigation-triggered saves, or draft restoration on next sign-in.

The candidate is directly supported by V1_REQ_015, which specifies a one-week training program immediately prior to go-live targeting at least 98% user completion of budget submissions within the deadline, and its usability phrasing is a reasonable restatement of that goal.

The candidate is directly supported by requirements stating that the system shall ingest the October month-end BDW snapshot and fully load 100% of organizational units and revenue/expense accounts existing in that snapshot before budgeting begins.

The candidate is directly supported by V1_REQ_008 and V1_REQ_009, which specify seven October month-end BDW files loaded within three hours one business day before budgeting kickoff and up to five BDW test loads per year, with the interfacing wording being a reasonable integration elaboration.

The source requirements describe online recycled-parts search performance, availability, and shop-floor usability, but do not state or clearly imply offline estimate building, queued searches during outages, or automatic synchronization within 5 minutes after reconnection.

The source explicitly states that the system shall interface with the ChoiceParts recycled parts catalog and ingest a nightly data feed, which directly supports interfacing with Choice Parts to obtain recycled parts data.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall run on corporate desktops supporting Internet Explorer version 5.5 or newer, which directly supports this candidate requirement.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that supervisor-level adjusters enter and save facility ratings on a 1–10 scale, which directly supports this candidate requirement.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that facility ratings must be saved within 5 seconds when a supervisor submits rating changes, which directly supports this candidate despite its redundant wording.
The sources require retaining search results and audits for later viewing, but they do not mention offline or connectivity-loss access, so this adds unsupported functionality.

The source explicitly states that the system shall be deployed, upgraded, and taken offline only during approved corporate maintenance windows, which directly supports that product maintenance falls within established corporate maintenance windows.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall adhere to corporate UI standards and color palette, which directly supports complying with the corporate color scheme.

The sources require on-demand audits, nightly ChoiceParts data ingestion, and later viewing of generated audits, but do not state or clearly imply automatic re-running of audits after refresh or notifications to assigned adjusters when scores change.

The sources explicitly require facility search using vehicle/location inputs and a user-set search radius in miles, and they return a preferred repair facility, so this is a reasonable paraphrase of those stated needs.
The source explicitly states the system shall maintain at least 98% uptime during scheduled online availability hours, and the 2% failure phrasing is a direct restatement of that requirement.
The source states that all adjusters can view current facility ratings, which directly supports a requirement that the user can choose to view preferred repair facility ratings.
The source explicitly states that the system shall support at least 1,500 concurrent users at initial launch, which directly supports the candidate's intent despite its awkward wording.
The sources state users should be able to resolve most issues without contacting the Corporate Support Center, but they do not explicitly require that the product be supported using that center.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall achieve at least 95% of adjusters and estimators rating it as easy to use, which directly supports this candidate requirement.

The source explicitly states that the system shall be usable and reliable in noisy and dirty repair shop environments when used by estimators on the shop floor, which directly supports this candidate requirement.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that audit results shall display the percentage of recycled parts used against available recycled parts, which directly supports including the percentage of available recycled parts used in the estimate.
The source explicitly states that adjusters can request an audit comparing the estimate’s recycled parts usage against available recycled parts, which directly supports this requirement.
The source explicitly states that the system shall implement Sarbanes–Oxley (SOX) controls, which directly supports the requirement that the product comply with Sarbanes-Oxley.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall incorporate protections to prevent malware infections and denial-of-service attacks, which directly supports preventing malicious attacks including denial of service.

The source supports adhering to approved maintenance/availability windows and 98% uptime during scheduled availability hours, but it does not support the specific and contradictory claim that downtime occurs only within '98% of the scheduled outages per the availability schedule.'

The sources mention corporate UI standards, maintenance-window deployment by Middleware, browser/platform constraints, security/compliance controls, and scaling needs, but they do not explicitly state or clearly imply a requirement to adhere to corporate Architecture guidelines.

The source explicitly states that supervisor-level adjusters can enter and save facility ratings and that the system shall save facility ratings, so saving preferred repair facility ratings is directly supported.

The sources explicitly require audit results to display 'total recycled parts available,' which directly supports including the number of available recycled parts from the retained search results in the audit report.

This is directly supported by the extracted requirements stating that the system returns available recycled parts with supplier information and retains the recycled parts search results tied to the estimate.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that recycled parts search results shall be returned within 15 seconds, which directly supports this candidate despite its redundant wording.

The source documents cover searching for, selecting, and assigning repair facilities but do not mention emailing facilities, notification timing, or sending claim/shop contact details after assignment.

The source explicitly states that the system shall display audit results and display the audit immediately upon request, so requiring the audit report to be displayed to the user is directly supported.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that all adjusters can view current facility ratings and that blank entries shall be shown if no ratings exist, which directly supports displaying a blank set of ratings when none are defined.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall validate all user inputs to prevent acceptance of invalid data, which directly supports the candidate’s intent.

The sources support that only supervisor-level adjusters may enter and save facility ratings, but they also explicitly state that all adjusters can view current facility ratings, so denying non-supervisors access to facility ratings is unsupported and contradicts the evidence.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that facility ratings are entered for two categories—attempted recycled parts usage and actual recycled parts usage—so the candidate is directly supported.

The source documents discuss searching, applying, and auditing recycled parts usage and availability, but they do not state or clearly imply any functionality to compare recycled parts to OEM prices or calculate/display cost savings totals.
The sources require generating, displaying, and retaining the recycled parts audit for viewing, but they do not mention exporting reports, PDF output, or digital signatures.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_012, which states the system shall automatically fall back to searching by the vehicle’s ZIP code within a 30-mile radius if no parts are found from the preferred suppliers.
This is directly supported by the extracted requirement that the system provide in-product help on approximately 90% of screens so users can resolve issues while operating the application.
The candidate is directly supported by V1_REQ_031, which states that at least 80% of estimators shall self-report improved productivity within one month of using the system.
The sources explicitly describe adjusters reviewing estimates in order to request audits and compare recycled parts usage, which clearly implies that the adjuster reviews the collision estimate.

The sources state that facility search uses the entered vehicle/location criteria and returns one preferred repair facility based on the highest performance rating and closest proximity, so this candidate is a supported paraphrase despite omitting the proximity detail.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_006, which states the system shall return a list of repair facilities within the search radius if no single clear preferred facility exists.

The sources mention accepting preferred suppliers as inputs for recycled parts search, but they do not support CSV import capability, shop-level preferred supplier list management, or pre-application validation of imported entries.

The source documents mention in-product help, training, UI standards, and browser support, but they do not state or clearly imply multilingual support, English/Spanish language selection, or persistence of a language preference per user profile.

This is directly supported by V1_REQ_007, which states that the system shall allow the adjuster to assign the chosen repair facility to the estimate and override the top recommended facility when finalizing the claim assignment.

The sources state that the system performs recycled parts searches and returns available recycled parts with supplier information to the estimator, which directly implies that the search results are retrieved by the system.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall comply with applicable state-by-state estimatics rules regarding recycled parts usage, which directly supports this candidate.

The sources require in-product help and reducing support contacts, but they do not state or clearly imply a built-in support ticket submission form, screenshot capture, or routing to the Corporate Support Center.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_018, which states that the audit shall be kept accessible for later viewing by other adjusters after it is generated.

The source explicitly states that the system shall accept a list of damaged parts and preferred suppliers as inputs for recycled parts search and return a list of available recycled parts, which directly supports this requirement.
The candidate directly matches V1_REQ_039, which states the system shall be architected to scale to support approximately 10,000 concurrent users within two years of launch.

The source explicitly states that the system shall generate and display recycled parts audit results within 10 seconds when an adjuster requests an audit, which directly supports this candidate despite its duplicated wording.

The sources explicitly require returning one preferred repair facility when a facility search is performed, so stating that the preferred repair facility shall be returned to the user is directly supported.

The source explicitly requires a two-day onsite training program for new users and includes usability goals indicating the system should be easy to use for adjusters and estimators, which reasonably supports that it should be easy to learn by those users with two days onsite training.

The source explicitly states the system shall deliver a 'clean, professional, and brand-aligned appearance' consistent with corporate UI standards, which directly supports the requirement that the product appear professional.

The sources state that supervisor-level adjusters can enter and save facility ratings, so a requirement that the user can choose to save preferred repair facility ratings is a supported paraphrase of that capability.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that facility search results shall be returned within 8 seconds, which directly supports a requirement that the preferred repair facility search complete within 8 seconds.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall comply with applicable insurance claims regulations and state-by-state rules, which directly supports this broader claims-processing compliance requirement.

The source explicitly states that the system shall achieve at least an 85% overall user satisfaction rating in surveys within the first month of deployment, which directly supports the candidate’s satisfaction requirement.
The source documents cover facility search, recommendation, selection, and assignment, but do not mention mapping, routing, or driving directions to repair facilities.
The source explicitly states that the system shall adhere to corporate UI standards and color palette, which directly supports compliance with corporate User Interface Guidelines.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_023, which states the system shall interface with the ChoiceParts recycled parts catalog via a nightly data feed at approximately 1:00 AM local time.

The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall accept a list of damaged parts and preferred suppliers as inputs for recycled parts search when an estimator builds an estimate, which directly supports the estimator searching using preferred parts suppliers.

The source explicitly requires adjusters to request an audit comparing the estimate’s recycled parts usage against available recycled parts and to display the audit results, which directly supports generating an audit report based on available recycled parts and the collision estimate.

The sources require user-facing searches, role-based access, and a ChoiceParts data interface, but they do not state or clearly imply exposing a secured REST API for authorized external systems to request facility or parts data by claim.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that audit results must display 'total recycled parts used,' which directly supports including that value in the audit report.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_002, which states the system shall accept street address, city, state, and ZIP code as inputs for facility search.

The source requirements mention help content, UI standards, usability, and browser support, but nowhere state or imply multilingual support, language preference selection, or Spanish localization of labels, help, and messages.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_003, which states the system shall automatically derive the ZIP code from the street address, city, and state if the ZIP code is missing during facility search.

This is directly supported by the extracted requirement that the system shall allow estimators to apply only recycled parts that are actually available to the estimate and prevent application of unavailable parts, which implies none can be applied when no available parts are returned.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that estimators can apply recycled parts to the estimate, specifically allowing application of available recycled parts when building the estimate.

The source only states that the system must run on existing corporate desktops without hardware upgrades for user access, not that it must run on existing hardware for all environments such as servers, test, or production.
This is directly supported by V1_REQ_021, which requires strict data isolation so users from one insurance carrier cannot access another carrier’s claim information.

The sources state that the system shall accept a list of damaged parts as input for recycled parts search when an estimator builds an estimate, which directly supports that the estimator searches for available recycled parts using damaged vehicle parts information.
The extracted requirements explicitly state that the system shall return a list of available recycled parts with associated supplier information when a recycled parts search is performed.

The source requirements mention performance, uptime, maintenance windows, and desktop/browser support, but do not state or clearly imply offline operation, queued saves, or post-reconnection synchronization behavior.
The source documents cover searching for and assigning a repair facility but do not mention sending notification emails to facilities or transmitting claim and adjuster details upon assignment.

The extracted requirement V1_REQ_017 explicitly states that audit results shall display 'an overall score summing line items,' which directly supports including the total audit score that sums individual line items.
